title: DATA_ENG 300: Data Engineering Studio
text:
Inclusivity
This course strives to be an inclusive learning community, respecting those of differing backgrounds and beliefs. As a community, we aim to be respectful to all students in this class, regardless of race, ethnicity, socio-economic status, religion, gender identity or sexual orientation.

title: DATA_ENG 300: Data Engineering Studio
text:
Office Hours
Weekly office hours are a dedicated time that the teaching team is available to answer your questions, discuss course content, and generally be of support. If none of the offered times in the syllabus works for you, please email the instructor to schedule an appointment.

title: DATA_ENG 300: Data Engineering Studio
text:
Late Days
You have 3 late days total for the quarter that you may use on homeworks. Late days are automatically applied.
A late day covers up to 24 hours after the deadline (or any part of that 24-hour period). Submissions covered by available late days are accepted with no penalty. No assignment is accepted more than 48 hours late, i.e., at most 2 late days can be used on one assignment.
If you have no late days remaining, late work will incur a penalty:

Less than 24 hours late: 20% deduction
24–48 hours late: 40% deduction
More than 48 hours late: not accepted

Please contact Professor Chan in the case of an emergency and discuss a plan.

title: DATA_ENG 300: Data Engineering Studio
text:
Communications
Canvas, where the syllabus, class announcements, exams, notes, and assignments are posted.
Piazza, where questions and discussions are directed.
Emails should lead with a title including DE300, followed by the matter of concern. Formal communications should be directed to emails, e.g., accommodation, progress discussion. The teaching team observes a 24-workhour response policy.

title: DATA_ENG 300: Data Engineering Studio
text:
Collaboration
You are encouraged to work with classmates on homeworks and labs. Discussing ideas, comparing approaches, and helping each other debug are all part of learning.
You are required to submit your own homeworks and labs written in your own words and presented in your own ways. Do not copy or paraphrase any other write-up other than your own, or share code files and solutions to others intended for direct usage.

title: DATA_ENG 300: Data Engineering Studio
text:
Generative AI Policy
GenAI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Copilot) may be used to support learning and productivity, but you are considered the engineer of record: you are fully responsible for the correctness, reproducibility, and integrity of all work you submit, and you must be able to explain and justify your design choices (e.g., schema/modeling, data processing choices, cost/performance, security/privacy).
Allowed uses: brainstorming pipeline designs and tradeoffs; debugging; drafting documentation; generating small code snippets that you can adapt and verify; identifying strengths and weaknesses of code.
Required disclosure: each submission must include an AI Usage note stating: (1) tool(s) used, (2) the key prompt(s), and (3) what you changed and how you verified the results. If none, write: “AI Usage: None.”
Not allowed: submitting work you do not understand or cannot explain; fabricating results, benchmarks, or citations; sharing credentials, private data, or any restricted datasets with GenAI tools.

title: DATA_ENG 300: Data Engineering Studio
text:
Accessibility
Northwestern University is committed to providing a supportive environment for students with disabilities. Should you anticipate or experience disability-related barriers in the academic setting, please contact AccessibleNU to move forward with the university’s established accommodation process. If you already have established accommodations with AccessibleNU, please let your instructor know as soon as possible, preferably within the first two weeks of the term, so we can work with you to implement your disability accommodations. Disability information, including academic accommodations, is confidential under the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act.

title: Handling missing data
text:
Classical categories of missing data
This document (briefly) introduces the concept, categorization, and treatment of missing data.
If we know how the missing data are generated, so-called missing data mechanisms, we can take into account why data are missing in data analysis.
A particularly useful (and wide-known) framework for missingness categorizes missingness into the following three categories (Little and Rudin, 2019):
Caveat… the category namings are… confusing
MCAR (missing completely at random): how data are missing is independent with any aspect of the data
MAR (missing at random): how data are missing depends on observed quantities, so-called predictors, or independent variables
MNAR (missing not at random): how data are missing depends on the unobserved quantities
Understanding the causes of missing data helps designing algorithms to handle missing data. However, in reality, we often do not know the causes, or multiple causes exist in producing a data set.
Cautionary note: Where the data are missing matters, i.e., is a response value missing, is a predictor value missing, or do we not know what our analysis questions are yet?

Example of missingness impacting analysis from Daniel et al. (2012), reproducing Figure 4 in the paper:








Effect of missingness mechanism on data analysis.




General rules for treating missing data:

Under MCAR, focusing on complete cases or employing imputation methods tend to be sufficient.
Under MAR, some methods are valid choices:

If the missingness is independent of the response conditional on the predictor, linear regression is still “valid”.
Imputation methods tend to introduce biases, depending on the data analysis methods.

Under MNAR, the missingness should be explicitly modeled.

e.g., censored observations in survival analysis.

title: Handling missing data
text:
Imputation methods
We introduce a (limited) list of imputation methods in this section, using the titanic dataset as an example:

!pip install missingno miceforest nineplot


import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
pd.set_option('display.precision', 3)

titanic = sns.load_dataset('titanic', cache=True, data_home='datasets/')


Identifying missingness
We have covered some basic ways how to identify missingness in the EDA module. Here is one other option with the help of missingno.

# !pip install missingno  
# in case you need to install the package

import missingno as msno


titanic.info()


import missingno as msno
# visualization of available/missing data
msno.bar(titanic)
msno.matrix(titanic)



Removing data
We have discussed that removing data is typically not ideal, but nothing stops us from doing that…

# retaining titanic dataset with complete data
titanic_agecomp = titanic.dropna(subset='age', how='any')
titanic_agecomp.info()


# comparing the correlation with / without missing data
corr = titanic.select_dtypes('number').corr()
corr_agecomp = titanic_agecomp.select_dtypes('number').corr()

# changes in correlation matrix with / without age missingness
corr - corr_agecomp


# one other possibility (that includes boolean)

# changes in correlation matrix with / without age missingness



Simple imputation with a constant

# !pip install scikit-learn
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer


titanic_constant = titanic.copy()

# set constant, completing data
constant_imputer = SimpleImputer(strategy='constant')
constant_imputer.fill_value = 100

titanic_constant['age'] = constant_imputer.fit_transform(titanic_constant[['age']])

# visualize completed data
sns.histplot(titanic_constant['age'])


titanic_median = titanic.copy()

# example with median, admitting only numeric columns


# visualize completed data
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1)
sns.histplot(titanic_median['age'], binwidth=5, alpha=0.3, color='k', label='imputed w/ median')
sns.histplot(titanic['age'], binwidth=5, alpha=0.3, color='purple', label='unimputed')
ax.legend()



Multiple imputation
Multiple imputation is a technique to generate multiple realizations of imputation values, often modeled by a probability distribution.
One method is the multiple imputation by chained equations, or MICE. miceforest uses LightBGM as its training algorithm for MICE.

# !pip install -U miceforest plotnine


import miceforest as mf

# miceforest requires a real matrix. 
# For illustrative purposes, we only use the numerical columns.

# set up a kernel to produce `num_datasets` imputed datasets
num_datasets = 4
kernel = mf.ImputationKernel(
    
)


# run the MICE algorithm
kernel.mice(iterations=10)



kernel.plot_imputed_distributions()


# retrieve completed data
titanic_mf = kernel.complete_data


# code for plotting mice completed dataset
def plot_hist(kernel, completed_titanic, original_titanic, num_plot):
    num_datasets = kernel.num_datasets
    num_plot = min(num_datasets, num_plot)
    for k in range(num_plot):  # 1, 2, 3, num_datasets
        sns.histplot(completed_titanic(dataset=k)['age'], binwidth=5, stat='probability', color='k', label='imputed sample', alpha=0.3)
    
    sns.histplot(original_titanic['age'], binwidth=5, stat='probability', color='purple', label='unimputed', alpha=0.2)
    
    plt.title('{:d} realizations of imputed age'.format(num_plot))
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()


# retrieving each imputed dataset (recall that M in MICE means multiple)
plot_hist(kernel=kernel, completed_titanic=titanic_mf, original_titanic=titanic, num_plot=num_datasets)


# comparing the original and imputed dataset
fig, ax = plt.subplots(ncols=5)

for k, axi in enumerate(ax.flatten()):
    if k < num_datasets:
        sns.boxplot(titanic_mf(dataset=k)['age'], ax=axi)
    else:
        sns.boxplot(titanic['age'], ax=axi, color='purple')

plt.tight_layout()

title: Handling missing data
text:
(Exercise) Imputation of tips
Consider the taxis dataset from seaborn, practice the imputation methods on the tip column for the dataset subtaxi_missing, given below:

Complete the data using

mean simple imputation,
median simple imputation,
k-nearest neighbor, and
predictive mean matching.

Compute the root mean squared error for each imputation method (because you know the truth). Which one is the best in this case?
Examine the correlation matrix among the numerical columns. Which methods preserve the correlation among variables?


import seaborn as sns

taxi = sns.load_dataset('taxis')
taxi.info()
subtaxi = taxi.loc[:, ['passengers', 'distance', 'fare', 'tip', 'tolls', 'total']]


import numpy as np
# create missingness
np.random.seed(42)
mask1 = subtaxi.total > 25
mask2 = np.random.rand(subtaxi.shape[0]) < 0.7

subtaxi_missing = subtaxi.copy()
subtaxi_missing.loc[mask1 & mask2, 'tip'] = np.nan


msno.matrix(subtaxi_missing.sort_values('total'))

Matrix completion
Matrix completion typically assumes some low-rank structure within the dataset.
Examples of usage:
crumbs: 7  Handling missing data in recommendation systems

recommendation system

title: Handling missing data in recommendation systems
text:
Alternating Least Squares
One particular algorithm that has garnered considerable interest is the Alternating Least Squares algorithm (Hastie et al. 2015).
Suppose we have a matrix \(R \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times m}\) that contains user ratings for items. The matrix completion or factorziation problem typically solves for some matrices \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{k\times n}, Y \in \mathbb{R}^{k\times m}\) such that \(R \approx X^\mathsf{T} Y\), through the optimization problem
\[\min_{X, Y} \ell(X, Y) := \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \|R - X^\mathsf{T} Y\|^2_F}_\text{recovery} + \underbrace{\frac{\lambda}{2} (\|X\|^2_F + \|Y\|^2_F)}_\text{regularization}.\]

Observation:

\(\ell(X, Y)\) is non-convex.
\(\ell(X, Y)\) is bi-convex, meaning that fixing either \(X\), or \(Y\) leads to a convex function.

The bi-convexity leads to an alternating algorithm (for a full \(R\)). But the missing locations do introduce nuances to the algorithm. See details in Theorem 3.1 of (Hastie et al. 2015).
crumbs: 7  Handling missing data in recommendation systems

title: Handling missing data in recommendation systems
text:
Bidirectional Encoder Representations (BERT) (Devlin et al. 2019)
Modern methods replace explicit low-rank assumptions with contextual representation learning, especially through masked prediction and self-attention.
Instead of predicting a single entry or factorizing the matrix directly, consider a full row as a sequence:
\[\mathbf{r}_i = (r_{i1}, r_{i2}, \dots, r_{im})\]
with missing entries treated as masked tokens.
The question becomes:
how do we predict a missing entry using all other entries in the row?
Consider a contextual encoder (BERT):
\[(\mathbf{h}_{i1}, \dots, \mathbf{h}_{im})=\mathrm{BERT}_\theta(\mathbf{r}_i)\]
where each \(\mathbf{h}_{ij}\) depends on both left and right context.
Prediction is made from a contextual embedding, e.g.,:
\[\hat{R}_{ij} = W \mathbf{h}_{ij}\]
Training is performed via masked reconstruction (randomly drop data in training):
\[\min_\theta \underbrace{\sum_{(i,j)\in\Omega_{\text{mask}}}\ell\big(\hat{R}_{ij}, R_{ij}\big)}_{\text{predict masked entries}}\]
Compare with classical matrix completion:
\[\min_{X,Y}\underbrace{\tfrac12 \|R - X^\mathsf{T}Y\|_F^2}_{\text{low-rank structure}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{\lambda}{2}(\|X\|_F^2+\|Y\|_F^2)}_{\text{regularization}}\]
crumbs: 7  Handling missing data in recommendation systems

title: Handling missing data in recommendation systems
section: Low rank adaptation (Shen et al., n.d.)
text:
Low rank adaptation (Shen et al., n.d.)
Modern methods consider “pre-trained” data and adaptation to previously trained models, especially in the large language model perspective.
Instead of viewing all data at once, consider the prediction of row \(i\) of the matrix \(R\):
\[ \mathbf{r}_i = W \underbrace{\mathbf{e}_i}_{\text{unit vec.}} \]
The question becomes, how do we learn the weight matrix \(W\)?
Suppose there is a pre-trained estimate \(W_0\), then matrix completion technique is used in estimating the residual:
\[ \min_{X, Y} \ell(X, Y; W_0) := \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \|R - W_0 - X^\mathsf{T} Y\|^2_F}_\text{fit residual} + \underbrace{\frac{\lambda}{2} (\|X\|^2_F + \|Y\|^2_F)}_\text{regularization} \]
Compare with:
\[ \min_{X, Y} \ell(X, Y) := \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \|R - X^\mathsf{T} Y\|^2_F}_\text{recovery} + \underbrace{\frac{\lambda}{2} (\|X\|^2_F + \|Y\|^2_F)}_\text{regularization}. \]






Devlin, Jacob, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, and Kristina Toutanova. 2019. “Bert: Pre-Training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding.” In Proceedings of the 2019 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies, Volume 1 (Long and Short Papers), 4171–86.


Hastie, Trevor, Rahul Mazumder, Jason D Lee, and Reza Zadeh. 2015. “Matrix Completion and Low-Rank SVD via Fast Alternating Least Squares.” The Journal of Machine Learning Research 16 (1): 3367–3402.


Shen, Yelong, Phillip Wallis, Zeyuan Allen-Zhu, Yuanzhi Li, Shean Wang, et al. n.d. “Lora: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models.”

Separation of compute from storage

Then why separate compute and storage?

title: Intro to parallelized computing
text:
a transactional database leverages its low-latency disk reads and high bandwidth.
even for distributed jobs, a map job locates the location of data and runs the map local to the data.



Availability of cloud computing and storage purchase

Cost: It is cheap to buy and host a server than to rent if the server is up 24/7, minus maintenance cost
Towards ephermerality: Spin up an instance, use, and stop when done.
Towards scalability: If you only need to run this titan job once a month, only pay for the resources then.

Data durability and availability

Fault tolerance: Replication of data increases availability and mitgates a write operation that destroys data.




Hybridization of separation and colocation
In reality, compute and storage are not either separated or colocated, e.g.,

AWS elastic mapreduce (EMR) and S3
Spark infrastructure dependence on RAM




Instances as a processing cache in an ephemeral Hadoop cluster, Fig 6.7 from Reis and Housley (2022)

title: Intro to parallelized computing
text:
Hadoop
Developed in 2006 to process data with the MapReduce programming model Dean and Ghemawat (2008), with the “big data” processing idea.
Main components of Apache Hadoop:

Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS): Manages large data sets running on commodity hardware (with data replication)
Yet Another Resource Negotiator (YARN)
Hadoop MapReduce: Splits data into blocks, distributes across different nodes, then runs task on each block in parallel
Hadoop Common (Hadoop Core): Common libraries and utilities

Why Hadoop is not enough?

Hadoop utilizes external storage. Read/write latency is high.
All codes must be restricted to a map and reduce constructs.

title: Intro to parallelized computing
text:
Spark
Main components:

Spark Core: Execution engine for resource coordination
Spark SQL
Machine Learning Library (MLlib)
Spark Streaming: Capability for processing streaming data
GraphX: Capability for processing graph-based data

Spark essentially tackles only where Hadoop does not perform well, by using an in-memory distributed computing engine,

making use of Resilient Distributed Dataset (RDD)
employing dynamic RAM in processing




MapReduce schema from Tian and Zhao (2015).








Dean, Jeffrey, and Sanjay Ghemawat. 2008. “MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters.” Communications of the ACM 51 (1): 107–13.


Reis, Joe, and Matt Housley. 2022. Fundamentals of Data Engineering. O’Reilly Media, Inc.


Tian, Wenhong, and Yong Zhao. 2015. “9 - Energy Efficiency Scheduling in Hadoop.” In Optimized Cloud Resource Management and Scheduling, edited by Wenhong Tian and Yong Zhao, 179–204. Boston: Morgan Kaufmann. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-801476-9.00009-4.

title: Resilient distributed datasets
section: Why RDD if we have dataframes?
text:
Why RDD if we have dataframes?

If the data at hand are more freeformed, using an RDD allows for storage of various types of objects.
Compared to dataframe, which will attempt (and fail) to find a common denominator to fit the data above.

title: Resilient distributed datasets
text:
Main ingredients of RDD manipulation
We cover three main building blocks for using RDDs, inheriting the concept of a MapReduce scheme.
Each of the following building blocks (functions) takes a functional input:

map()
filter()
reduce()


map through an example
map() applies the given function to each element of the RDD.

from py4j.protocol import Py4JJavaError

def add_one(value):
    return value + 1

collection_rdd_p1 = collection_rdd.map(add_one)


collection_rdd_p1.collect()


try: 
    print(collection_rdd_p1.collect())
except Py4JJavaError as e:
    print(e)

# You'll get one of the following:
# TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "int") to str
# TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'dict' and 'int'
# TypeError: can only concatenate tuple (not "int") to tuple












Applying add_one() to each element of RDD through map() (Fig 8.2 in Rioux, 2022).



Quick note:

Why did the line throw an error?
When was the error thrown?

A potential fix:

def safer_add_one(value):
    try:
        return value + 1
    except TypeError:
        return value

collection_rdd_p1_again = collection_rdd.map(safer_add_one)


collection_rdd_p1_again.collect()

Lesson here:

PySpark does not warn you about the content of the RDD.
As the developer, we are responsible for how to deal with the data given to an RDD.



filter through an example
filter() takes a function that returns True/False based on any conditions.

collection_rdd_filter = collection_rdd.filter(
    lambda elem: isinstance(elem, (float, int))
)


print(collection_rdd_filter.collect())









Applying filter() to the RDD (Fig 8.3 in Rioux, 2022).



A word about lambda function








The use of lambda function (Rioux, 2022).





reduce through an example
reduce() summarizes the RDD by sequentially applying the given function.

similar to groupby() in a dataframe.


from operator import add

collection_rdd2 = sc.parallelize([4, 7, 9.2, 5.6, -20])


collection_rdd2.reduce(add)


# collection_rdd2.reduce(
#     lambda a, b: a + b
# )









Applying add through reduce() to the RDD (Fig 8.4 in Rioux, 2022).



Warnings about reduce()

What functions are reasonable for reduce()?
commutative operation
associative operation

Additional Note:
A dataframe is actually an RDD, e.g.,

df = spark.createDataFrame([[1], [2], [3]], schema=["column"])
 
print(df.rdd)
 
print(df.rdd.collect())

title: Machine learning examples in PySpark
text:
Linear regression refresher
To fit a linear regression (without any penalty or regularization) can be done via the least-squares estimation:
\[\min_\beta~\ell(\beta) = \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X} \beta \|_2^2,\]
The corresponding analytical solution can be found by setting the partial derivatives to zero: \[\frac{\partial \ell(\beta)}{\partial \beta} \equiv 0 \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad \widehat{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{y}.\]
Why do we need to think about parallelization at all?
Say we have \(n\) data points, i.e. \((\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)_{i=1}^n\). Take a look at the dimension of the quantities of interest: \[\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (d+1)}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n.\]
What are the major computational tasks?
The major computations involve three operations:
Rationale:
Lecture continues with examples linked above.

title: Machine learning examples in PySpark
text:
Multiplication: \(\mathbf{X}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{X} = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^\mathsf{T},\)
Multiplication: \(\mathbf{X}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{x_i} y_i,\)
Inversion of matrix: \((\mathbf{X}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{y}.\)



In the case where \(n\) is extremely large, the multiplication and summation, steps (1) and (2), are slow.
Since the inversion of matrix is driven by the size of \(\mathbf{X}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{X}\), which is typically a relatively small \((d+1) \times (d+1)\) matrix. Step (3) is relatively cheap in terms of computation.

title: Fitting and Plotting a Random Forest Model with PySpark on EC2
text:
What we want to build

Spark session
spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .appName("ec2-random-forest-regression-demo")
    .master("local[*]")
    .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "8")
    .getOrCreate()
)
local[*] tells Spark to use all available CPU cores on the EC2 instance.
This is not a multi-node Spark cluster. It is single-machine Spark.


Synthetic data
The synthetic dataset has:
price =
    base price
    + bedroom effect
    + bathroom effect
    + square-footage effect
    - age penalty
    - distance penalty
    + noise
The random forest model should usually discover that sqft, distance_to_city_miles, and age_years matter.


VectorAssembler
Spark MLlib models usually do not accept many separate feature columns directly. Instead, they expect a single vector column.
This:
assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=feature_cols,
    outputCol="features",
    handleInvalid="skip",
)
turns columns like this:
bedrooms | bathrooms | sqft | age_years | distance_to_city_miles
into one column like this:
features
The resulting features column is what RandomForestRegressor requires.


Random forest model
The model is defined here:
rf = RandomForestRegressor(
    featuresCol="features",
    labelCol="price",
    predictionCol="prediction",
    numTrees=80,
    maxDepth=8,
    seed=42,
)
A random forest is an ensemble of decision trees. Each tree is created with a subset of the full dataset. The final prediction averages across the trees, which usually makes the model more stable than a single decision tree.
Important parameters:



Parameter
Meaning




numTrees
Number of trees in the forest


maxDepth
Maximum depth of each tree


featuresCol
Feature vector column


labelCol
Target column


predictionCol
Output prediction column


seed
Makes results easier to reproduce





Pipeline
The pipeline joins preprocessing and modeling:
pipeline = Pipeline(stages=[assembler, rf])
model = pipeline.fit(train_df)
Due to the pipeline usage, the fitted model contains both:

The trained feature assembler step.
The trained random forest model.

title: Fitting and Plotting a Random Forest Model with PySpark on EC2
text:
Plotting

Converting to Pandas
Spark DataFrames are distributed. Matplotlib expects local arrays.
So this is the common pattern:
plot_pdf = (
    predictions
    .select("price", "prediction")
    .sample(fraction=0.25, seed=7)
    .limit(1000)
    .toPandas()
)
Do not call .toPandas() on a huge Spark DataFrame.
Instead:

Select only the columns you need.
Sample or limit the rows.
Convert the small result to pandas.
Plot locally.



Actual vs. predicted plot
The diagonal line represents perfect predictions.
Points near the line are good.
Points far from the line are mistakes.
plt.scatter(plot_pdf["price"], plot_pdf["prediction"], alpha=0.35)
plt.plot([min_val, max_val], [min_val, max_val], linestyle="--")
If the model is learning well, the scatter should form a rough diagonal band.


Feature importance plot
Random forest models expose feature importances:
rf_model = model.stages[-1]
importances = rf_model.featureImportances.toArray()
Because the random forest is the last stage in the pipeline, model.stages[-1] retrieves the fitted random forest model.
Then we match each importance score back to the original feature name:
importance_pdf = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "feature": feature_cols,
        "importance": importances,
    }
)

title: Fitting and Plotting a Random Forest Model with PySpark on EC2
section: (Appendix) Full Random Forest on EC2 PySpark script
text:
(Appendix) Full Random Forest on EC2 PySpark script
Create the script:
cat > rf_housing_demo.py <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, rand, round as spark_round

from pyspark.ml import Pipeline
from pyspark.ml.feature import VectorAssembler
from pyspark.ml.regression import RandomForestRegressor, RandomForestRegressionModel
from pyspark.ml.evaluation import RegressionEvaluator


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Start Spark
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .appName("ec2-random-forest-regression-demo")
    .master("local[*]")
    .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "8")
    .getOrCreate()
)

spark.sparkContext.setLogLevel("WARN")

output_dir = Path("outputs")
output_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Build a synthetic housing dataset
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# Imagine each row is a home listing.
#
# Features:
# - bedrooms
# - bathrooms
# - sqft
# - age_years
# - distance_to_city_miles
#
# Label:
# - price
#
# The price formula below is intentionally simple, but includes noise.
# A random forest should learn the broad pattern without us telling it
# the formula.

n = 5000

df = (
    spark.range(0, n)
    .withColumn("bedrooms", (1 + (rand(seed=1) * 5)).cast("int"))
    .withColumn("bathrooms", spark_round(1 + rand(seed=2) * 3, 1))
    .withColumn("sqft", spark_round(650 + rand(seed=3) * 3350, 0))
    .withColumn("age_years", spark_round(rand(seed=4) * 80, 0))
    .withColumn("distance_to_city_miles", spark_round(rand(seed=5) * 40, 1))
    .withColumn("noise", spark_round((rand(seed=6) - 0.5) * 50000, 2))
    .withColumn(
        "price",
        spark_round(
            50000
            + col("bedrooms") * 18000
            + col("bathrooms") * 22000
            + col("sqft") * 210
            - col("age_years") * 1200
            - col("distance_to_city_miles") * 3500
            + col("noise"),
            2,
        ),
    )
    .drop("noise")
)

print("\nSample data:")
df.show(5, truncate=False)

print("\nSchema:")
df.printSchema()


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Define features and label
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

feature_cols = [
    "bedrooms",
    "bathrooms",
    "sqft",
    "age_years",
    "distance_to_city_miles",
]

label_col = "price"

assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=feature_cols,
    outputCol="features",
    handleInvalid="skip",
)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Split into train and test data
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

train_df, test_df = df.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

print(f"\nTraining rows: {train_df.count()}")
print(f"Test rows:     {test_df.count()}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Fit a random forest regressor
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# A few important parameters:
#
# - numTrees: number of decision trees in the forest
# - maxDepth: maximum depth of each tree
# - seed: makes the demo repeatable
# - featuresCol: vector column created by VectorAssembler
# - labelCol: target column we want to predict

rf = RandomForestRegressor(
    featuresCol="features",
    labelCol=label_col,
    predictionCol="prediction",
    numTrees=80,
    maxDepth=8,
    seed=42,
)

pipeline = Pipeline(stages=[assembler, rf])

model = pipeline.fit(train_df)

print("\nModel fitted.")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Score the test set
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

predictions = model.transform(test_df)

print("\nPredictions:")
predictions.select(
    "bedrooms",
    "bathrooms",
    "sqft",
    "age_years",
    "distance_to_city_miles",
    "price",
    "prediction",
).show(10, truncate=False)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Evaluate model quality
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

rmse_evaluator = RegressionEvaluator(
    labelCol=label_col,
    predictionCol="prediction",
    metricName="rmse",
)

r2_evaluator = RegressionEvaluator(
    labelCol=label_col,
    predictionCol="prediction",
    metricName="r2",
)

mae_evaluator = RegressionEvaluator(
    labelCol=label_col,
    predictionCol="prediction",
    metricName="mae",
)

rmse = rmse_evaluator.evaluate(predictions)
r2 = r2_evaluator.evaluate(predictions)
mae = mae_evaluator.evaluate(predictions)

print("\nEvaluation metrics:")
print(f"RMSE: ${rmse:,.2f}")
print(f"MAE:  ${mae:,.2f}")
print(f"R^2:  {r2:.4f}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Convert a small sample to pandas for plotting
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# Spark is good for distributed processing.
# Matplotlib is local.
#
# So the usual pattern is:
#
## Use Spark to train and score the large dataset.
## Pull down only the small result needed for plotting.

plot_pdf = (
    predictions
    .select("price", "prediction")
    .sample(fraction=0.25, seed=7)
    .limit(1000)
    .toPandas()
)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Plot actual vs. predicted prices
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.scatter(plot_pdf["price"], plot_pdf["prediction"], alpha=0.35)

min_val = min(plot_pdf["price"].min(), plot_pdf["prediction"].min())
max_val = max(plot_pdf["price"].max(), plot_pdf["prediction"].max())

plt.plot([min_val, max_val], [min_val, max_val], linestyle="--")
plt.xlabel("Actual price")
plt.ylabel("Predicted price")
plt.title("Random Forest: Actual vs. Predicted Housing Prices")
plt.tight_layout()

actual_vs_predicted_path = output_dir / "actual_vs_predicted.png"
plt.savefig(actual_vs_predicted_path, dpi=150)
plt.close()

print(f"\nSaved plot: {actual_vs_predicted_path}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Plot feature importances
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

rf_model = model.stages[-1]

importances = rf_model.featureImportances.toArray()

importance_pdf = (
    pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "feature": feature_cols,
            "importance": importances,
        }
    )
    .sort_values("importance", ascending=True)
)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.barh(importance_pdf["feature"], importance_pdf["importance"])
plt.xlabel("Importance")
plt.title("Random Forest Feature Importances")
plt.tight_layout()

feature_importance_path = output_dir / "feature_importances.png"
plt.savefig(feature_importance_path, dpi=150)
plt.close()

print(f"Saved plot: {feature_importance_path}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Save the model
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

model_path = output_dir / "rf_housing_pipeline_model"

# Overwrite if re-running the script.
model.write().overwrite().save(str(model_path))

print(f"Saved model: {model_path}")


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Load the model back
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

loaded_model = model.load(str(model_path))
loaded_predictions = loaded_model.transform(test_df.limit(5))

print("\nPredictions from loaded model:")
loaded_predictions.select("price", "prediction").show(truncate=False)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
## Stop Spark
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------

spark.stop()
PY
Run it:
python rf_housing_demo.py
You should end up with this folder:
outputs/
├── actual_vs_predicted.png
├── feature_importances.png
└── rf_housing_pipeline_model/

title: MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration
text:
Why Workflow Orchestration Exists
Modern data systems are rarely one-step scripts.
They usually require:
Without orchestration, workflows become fragile collections of scripts, jobs, and manual checks.
crumbs: 12  MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration

title: MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration
text:
Ingesting data from multiple systems
Validating data quality
Transforming raw data into useful models
Loading outputs into warehouses or applications
Notifying downstream consumers
Retrying failures safely




Example: A Simple Data Pipeline





graph LR
  A["Download data"] --> B["Validate schema"]
  B --> C[Transform]
  C --> D[Load table]
  D --> E[Send alert]






This looks simple, but production adds complexity:

What if validation fails?
What if the warehouse is temporarily unavailable?
What if yesterday’s data needs to be reprocessed?
What if two tasks can run in parallel?




Orchestration Solves Coordination Problems
Workflow orchestration coordinates:

Task order
Scheduling
Dependencies
Retries
Failure handling
Parallelism
State tracking
Observability

The orchestrator answers:

What should run next, when, and under what conditions?




Orchestration vs Execution








Concept
Main responsibility
Example




Orchestration
Coordinates workflow logic
Airflow decides task order


Execution
Performs actual work
Spark transforms a dataset



Airflow is primarily an orchestrator.
It can run Python code, but it is not designed to be a distributed compute engine for large-scale data processing.



Mental Model





flowchart LR
  A[Airflow DAG] --> B[Submit Spark Job]
  A --> C[Trigger Glue Job]
  A --> D[Call API]
  A --> E[Run Python Task]

  B --> F[Compute Happens Elsewhere]
  C --> F
  D --> F
  E --> G[Compute Happens on Worker]






Airflow coordinates the workflow; the compute may happen on workers or in external systems.
crumbs: 12  MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration

title: MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration
text:
What Is Apache Airflow?
Apache Airflow is an open-source platform to:

Author workflows as Python code
Schedule workflows
Manage dependencies
Retry failed tasks
Monitor task state
Backfill historical runs

Airflow models workflows as DAGs.
A DAG is the central abstraction.


DAG Theory
A DAG is a Directed Acyclic Graph.



Term
Meaning in Airflow




Directed
Edges have direction; order matters


Acyclic
No circular dependencies


Graph
Tasks connected by dependencies


Node
A task


Edge
A dependency






Valid DAG





flowchart LR
  Extract --> Transform --> Load






This is valid because dependencies move in one direction and never loop back.



Invalid DAG: Cycle





flowchart LR
  A[Task A] --> B[Task B]
  B --> C[Task C]
  C --> A






This is invalid because Task A depends indirectly on itself.
Airflow cannot create a stable execution order from this graph.



Why Acyclic Matters
Acyclic graphs allow Airflow to determine:

Which tasks have no unmet dependencies
Which tasks can run now
Which tasks must wait
Which tasks are blocked by failures
When the whole DAG run is complete

Cycles make this impossible.



Parallelism from DAG Structure





flowchart TD
  A[Extract source data] --> B[Transform sales]
  A --> C[Transform inventory]
  A --> D[Transform customers]
  B --> E[Load warehouse]
  C --> E
  D --> E






Tasks B, C, and D can run in parallel after A succeeds.
Parallelism is determined by both:

DAG structure
Runtime concurrency limits




DAGs Enable Fault Isolation
If one task fails, Airflow can isolate the failure.





flowchart LR
  A[Extract] --> B[Transform]
  B --> C[Load]
  B --> D[Quality Report]






If D fails, Airflow does not necessarily need to rerun A, B, and C.
This is one reason task granularity matters.
crumbs: 12  MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration

title: MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration
text:
Airflow Core Concepts



Concept
Meaning




DAG
Workflow definition


DAG Run
One execution of a DAG


Task
Unit of work in a DAG


Task Instance
One execution of a task in a specific DAG Run


Operator
Template for a task action


XCom
Small cross-task message


Scheduler
Decides what should run


Worker
Executes tasks





DAG vs DAG Run
A DAG is the blueprint.
A DAG Run is a specific execution of that blueprint.





flowchart TD
  D[DAG: daily_sales_pipeline] --> R1[DAG Run: 2026-05-10]
  D --> R2[DAG Run: 2026-05-11]
  D --> R3[DAG Run: 2026-05-12]









Task vs Task Instance
A task is defined once in code.
A task instance is created each time the task runs.





flowchart TD
  T[Task: validate_sales] --> I1[Task Instance for 2026-05-10]
  T --> I2[Task Instance for 2026-05-11]
  T --> I3[Task Instance for 2026-05-12]






This distinction is essential for debugging Airflow state.



Airflow Architecture





flowchart LR
  DAGs[DAG Files] --> Scheduler[Scheduler]
  Scheduler --> Metadata[(Metadata DB)]
  Scheduler --> Queue[Executor Queue]
  Queue --> Workers[Workers]
  Workers --> Metadata
  Webserver[Webserver / UI] --> Metadata






The scheduler decides what should run.
Workers execute tasks.
The metadata database stores the truth about workflow state.



Component: Scheduler
The scheduler is the orchestration brain.
Responsibilities:

Parse DAG files
Create DAG Runs
Evaluate dependencies
Queue runnable tasks
Respect concurrency limits
Update scheduling state

Important mental model:

The scheduler continuously evaluates whether task instances are ready to run.




Component: Metadata Database
The metadata database stores:

DAG definitions and serialized metadata
DAG Run state
Task Instance state
Retry history
XCom values
Connection and variable metadata

Airflow is a state-driven system.
The metadata database is the source of truth.



Component: Workers
Workers execute tasks.
Tasks may:

Run Python functions
Execute shell commands
Call APIs
Submit jobs to AWS Glue, EMR, ECS, Lambda, or Spark
Read/write to databases or object storage

Workers report state and logs back to Airflow infrastructure.



Component: Webserver / UI
The Airflow UI helps users inspect:

DAG list
Graph view
Grid view
Logs
Import errors
XComs
Retries
Task duration
Connections, variables, and pools

The UI mostly reads state from the metadata database.



Executor Concept
The executor determines how Airflow launches tasks.



Executor
Common use




SequentialExecutor
Local single-task testing


LocalExecutor
Parallel local execution


CeleryExecutor
Distributed workers


KubernetesExecutor
Task-per-pod execution



MWAA abstracts much of this infrastructure away from the user.
crumbs: 12  MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration

title: MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration
text:
Why MWAA?
Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow is AWS’s managed Airflow service.
MWAA reduces the need to operate:

Scheduler infrastructure
Webserver infrastructure
Worker infrastructure
Metadata database
Logging integration
Scaling infrastructure
AWS networking integration



MWAA Architecture





flowchart TB
  S3[S3 Bucket: DAGs / plugins / requirements] --> MWAA[MWAA Environment]
  MWAA --> CW[CloudWatch Logs]
  MWAA --> RDS[(Managed Metadata DB)]
  IAM[IAM Execution Role] --- MWAA
  VPC[Customer VPC] --- MWAA
  MWAA --> AWS[AWS Services]






MWAA connects Airflow to AWS-native infrastructure.



What MWAA Manages
MWAA manages:

Airflow scheduler
Airflow webserver
Worker environment
Metadata database infrastructure
Logs to CloudWatch
Environment scaling integration
Airflow environment lifecycle

You still manage:

DAG code
Dependencies
IAM permissions
S3 deployment structure
VPC connectivity choices
Airflow configuration choices




MWAA Deployment Model





sequenceDiagram
  participant Dev as Developer
  participant S3 as S3 Bucket
  participant MWAA as MWAA Scheduler
  participant Worker as MWAA Worker
  participant Logs as CloudWatch

  Dev->>S3: Upload DAG file
  MWAA->>S3: Sync and parse DAGs
  MWAA->>Worker: Queue runnable task
  Worker->>Logs: Stream task logs
  Worker->>MWAA: Report task state






The DAG file is deployed to S3, then MWAA parses and schedules it.



MWAA and S3
Typical S3 layout:
s3://your-mwaa-bucket/
  dags/
    example_dag.py
  plugins/
    custom_plugin.zip
  requirements.txt
S3 is the source location for DAGs and environment dependencies.
Common issue:

The DAG is uploaded, but Airflow does not show it because of path, parsing, or dependency errors.




MWAA and IAM
IAM controls what your Airflow environment can access.
Examples:

Read/write S3 objects
Start AWS Glue jobs
Access Secrets Manager
Publish CloudWatch logs
Query Redshift or Athena

Many MWAA task failures are not Airflow logic failures.
They are permissions failures.



MWAA and VPC
VPC configuration controls network reachability.
Airflow tasks may need access to:

Private databases
Internal APIs
VPC endpoints
AWS services
Internet endpoints

Common failure:

A task is logically correct but cannot reach the service it needs because of subnet, route table, endpoint, or security group configuration.
crumbs: 12  MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration

title: MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration
text:
Airflow Execution Lifecycle





flowchart TB
  A[Parse DAG File] --> B[Register DAG]
  B --> C[Create DAG Run]
  C --> D[Resolve Dependencies]
  D --> E[Queue Task Instance]
  E --> F[Worker Executes Task]
  F --> G[Persist State]
  F --> H[Emit Logs]






Use this lifecycle to debug almost every Airflow issue.


Debugging by Lifecycle Stage








Stage
Common symptom
First place to check




Parse DAG
DAG missing
Import Errors


Schedule DAG Run
No run appears
schedule, start_date, pause state


Queue task
Task stuck queued
workers, pools, concurrency


Execute task
Task fails
task logs


Access AWS service
Access denied
IAM execution role


Network call
Timeout
VPC, route, endpoint, security group




Discuss with a neighbor:

What is different between Airflow and PySpark?
What breaks if a workflow graph contains a cycle?
Why does Airflow need a metadata database?
What does MWAA manage, and what does the DAG author still own?
Where would you look first if a DAG does not appear in the UI?
crumbs: 12  MWAA and Airflow Workflow Orchestration

title: Project Specification
text:
Project proposal
Develop a proposal that includes your topic, the selection of relevant dataset(s), and a plan to answer questions of interests. Keep in mind the timeline of the quarter and set achievable goals.

Guidelines for proposal
Review the guidelines below carefully.
Overarching theme: Design a data process that involves ingesting data, preprocessing, analysis, and service. Consider a process that can scale and can be reused given data update.
The proposal should follow this format (5 points):

Include full names of all teammates.
Between 1 to 2 pages, single-spaced, 11-point type, 1-inch margins.
Do not include graphics.
Decide on the name of your group. A pdf named [GroupName]_proposal.pdf should be submitted.

The proposal should cover the following components (but in a narrative format, not Q/A):

Introduction (12 points): Introduce your project and motivation, e.g.,

What is the main issue you are interested in?
Why is this topic important?
In what way does this project provide a solution?

Data source(s) (10 points): Describe the data sources you have chosen, e.g.,

How can the data be retrieved?
How are the data related to the topic?
State the amount of data you will be working with.

Goal definitions (18 points): State two to three goals you are interested in achieving with the selected data sources.
As you tackle the goals, you should use at least one of the data engineering techniques covered in class:

Missing data imputation
Database system (relational or non-relational)
Distributed computing framework (e.g., pyspark)
For each goal, answer the following:
How do you intend to achieve your goal?
How do you intend to use the above techniques?
What can you answer (and not answer) with the proposed datasets?

Reference (5 points): Provide valid references for your data source(s). (Reference does not count toward the page limit.)

For example, using APA guidelines, the MIMIC-III database can be cited as > Johnson, A., Pollard, T., & Mark, R. (2016). MIMIC-III Clinical Database (version 1.4). PhysioNet. https://doi.org/10.13026/C2XW26.
or the original article > Johnson, A. E., Pollard, T. J., Shen, L., Lehman, L. W. H., Feng, M., Ghassemi, M., … & Mark, R. G. (2016). MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database. Scientific data, 3(1), 1-9.

Up to fifteen minutes.

Note that while the evaluation is primarily for the entire group, evaluation may differ among students if significant discrepancy is observed. You may refer to the sample reference rubric for details.

Prepare a project report to clearly outline your project choice and its importance, your approach to achieving the set of goals, your results, and a summary.
The report should follow these guidlines:

Include full names of all teammates.

This is a reminder that it is an academic violation to include your name on any work you have not contributed or performed.

Up to 15 pages (not including Appendix), single-spaced, 11-point type, 1-inch margins.

Include all of your code in a zipped folder in your submission. Make sure your codes and code organization are understandable.

What is the main issue you are interested in?
Why is this topic important?
In what way does this project provide a solution?

Data source(s) (5 points): Describe the data sources you have chosen, e.g.,

How can the data be retrieved?
How are the data related to the topic?
State the amount of data you will be working with.

Goals and approaches (60 points): State the specific goals you are attempting to achieve with the selected data sources. For each goal, provide the approach in achieving said goal, its implementation, and your results. Use any graph, table, or other visualization to support your narrative.
Summary (10 points): Provide a brief summary of your project, and describe any loose ends or future opportunities.
Generative AI statement and reference (10 points):

Generative AI statement: If you have, in any way, employed the use of generative AI tools, report all usage according to the syllabus. This statement does not count toward the page limit.
Reference: Provide valid references for your data source(s). (Does not count toward the page limit.)

For example, using APA guidelines, the MIMIC-III database can be cited as > Johnson, A., Pollard, T., & Mark, R. (2016). MIMIC-III Clinical Database (version 1.4). PhysioNet. https://doi.org/10.13026/C2XW26.
or the original article > Johnson, A. E., Pollard, T. J., Shen, L., Lehman, L. W. H., Feng, M., Ghassemi, M., … & Mark, R. G. (2016). MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database. Scientific data, 3(1), 1-9.


Code legibility and organization (10 points): Give a brief summary or provide a README to use and evaluate your code. (Does not count toward the page limit.)

title: Presentation reference rubric
text:
Thorough Understanding
Good Understanding
Basic Understanding
Poor/No Understanding




Organization
Organizational pattern (specific introduction and conclusion, sequenced material within the body, and transitions) is clearly and consistently observable and is skillful and makes the content of the presentation cohesive.
Organizational pattern (specific introduction and conclusion, sequenced material within the body, and transitions) is clearly and consistently observable within the presentation.
Organizational pattern (specific introduction and conclusion, sequenced material within the body, and transitions) is intermittently observable within the presentation.
Organizational pattern (specific introduction and conclusion, sequenced material within the body, and transitions) is not observable within the presentation.


Language
Language choices are memorable, compelling, and enhance the effectiveness of the presentation. Language in presentation is appropriate to audience.
Language choices are thoughtful and generally support the effectiveness of the presentation. Language in presentation is appropriate to audience.
Language choices are mundane and commonplace and partially support the effectiveness of the presentation. Language in presentation is appropriate to audience.
Language choices are unclear and minimally support the effectiveness of the presentation. Language in presentation is not appropriate to audience.


Delivery
Delivery techniques (posture, gesture, eye contact, and vocal expressiveness) make the presentation compelling, and speaker appears polished and confident.
Delivery techniques (posture, gesture, eye contact, and vocal expressiveness) make the presentation interesting, and speaker appears comfortable.
Delivery techniques (posture, gesture, eye contact, and vocal expressiveness) make the presentation understandable, and speaker appears tentative.
Delivery techniques (posture, gesture, eye contact, and vocal expressiveness) detract from the understandability of the presentation, and speaker appears uncomfortable.


Supporting Material
A variety of types of supporting materials (explanations, illustrations, statistics) make appropriate reference to information or analysis that significantly supports the presentation or establishes the presenter’s credibility/authority on the topic.
Supporting materials (explanations, illustrations, statistics) make appropriate reference to information or analysis that generally supports the presentation or establishes the presenter’s credibility/authority on the topic.
Supporting materials (explanations, illustrations, statistics) make appropriate reference to information or analysis that partially supports the presentation or establishes the presenter’s credibility/authority on the topic.
Insufficient supporting materials (explanations, illustrations, statistics) make reference to information or analysis that minimally supports the presentation or establishes the presenter’s credibility/authority on the topic.


Central Message
Central message is precisely stated and strongly supported.
Central message is clear and consistent with the supporting material.
Central message is basically understandable but is not memorable.
Central message can be deduced, but is not explicitly stated in the presentation.

title: Setting up access to Keyspace in Jupyter notebook
text:
Sample code for connection

Install cassandra-sigv4 via the following command:


!pip install cassandra-sigv4


Set up a boto3 session and a Cassandra cluster (the Python way of interacting with AWS).


from cassandra.cluster import Cluster
from ssl import SSLContext, PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2 , CERT_REQUIRED
import boto3
from cassandra_sigv4.auth import SigV4AuthProvider

ssl_context = SSLContext(PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2)
ssl_context.load_verify_locations('keyspaces-bundle.pem')
ssl_context.verify_mode = CERT_REQUIRED

<positron-console-cell-5>:6: DeprecationWarning: ssl.PROTOCOL_TLSv1_2 is deprecated



import pandas as pd
access_key = pd.read_csv('../admin/de300-keyspaces_accessKeys.csv')


# use this if you want to use Boto to set the session parameters.
boto_session = boto3.Session(aws_access_key_id="AKIAYAAO5HRMC6K3K34T",
                             aws_secret_access_key=access_key['Secret access key'].values[0],
                            #  aws_session_token="AQoDYXdzEJr...<remainder of token>",
                             region_name="us-east-1")
auth_provider = SigV4AuthProvider(boto_session)

cluster = Cluster(['cassandra.us-east-1.amazonaws.com'], 
                  ssl_context=ssl_context, 
                  auth_provider=auth_provider,
                  port=9142)
session = cluster.connect()
r = session.execute('select * from system_schema.keyspaces')
print(r.current_rows)

title: Setting Up PySpark on EC2
text:
Overview
This guide walks through setting up PySpark on a single Amazon EC2 instance. It is intended for development, testing, lightweight analysis, and small jobs.
For production-scale Spark workloads, consider Amazon EMR, AWS Glue, Databricks, or another managed Spark platform instead of hand-rolling a standalone EC2 setup.

title: Setting Up PySpark on EC2
text:
Java not found
Check Java:
java -version
If missing:
sudo dnf install -y java-17-amazon-corretto-devel

title: Setting Up PySpark on EC2
text:
PySpark cannot find Python
Add this to ~/.bashrc:
export PYSPARK_PYTHON=python3
Reload the shell config:
source ~/.bashrc

title: Setting Up PySpark on EC2
text:
EC2 runs out of memory
Use a larger instance or limit Spark driver memory:
spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .appName("memory-limited-job")
    .master("local[*]")
    .config("spark.driver.memory", "4g")
    .getOrCreate()
)

Overview
This guide walks through setting up MWAA Environment on AWS.
crumbs: 18  Setting Up MWAA Environment on EC2

title: Setting Up MWAA Environment on EC2
text:
Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket
MWAA uses S3 to store your DAGs, plugins, and requirements.

Go to S3 in the AWS Console and create a new bucket (e.g., de300-airflow-lastnames).
Versioning should be enabled by default on the bucket. Double check, this is required by MWAA.
Create the following folder structure inside the bucket:

my-mwaa-bucket/
├── dags/
├── plugins/        # optional
└── requirements.txt  # optional
crumbs: 18  Setting Up MWAA Environment on EC2

title: Setting Up MWAA Environment on EC2
text:
Step 2: Create the MWAA Environment

Navigate to Amazon MWAA in the AWS Console and click Create environment.
Fill in the configuration:




Setting
Value




Name
de300-airflow-lastnames


Airflow version
Latest stable (e.g., 2.9.x)


S3 bucket
s3://YOUR-BUCKET/


DAGs folder
s3://YOUR-BUCKET/dags/


Execution role
arn:.....MWAA.....


VPC / Subnets
Choose the VPC that indicates MWAA




Under Network access, choose:

Public — accessible over the internet (simpler for getting started)

Change Maximum number of workers to 4.
Click Create environment and wait 20–30 minutes for provisioning.
